conversation_context: The user asked the agent to set up a full CI/CD pipeline that integrates GitHub, runs pytest, builds a Docker image, pushes it to ECR, and deploys to ECS via Terraform. The agent completed the task using 8 sequential tool calls (repo read, workflow file write, Dockerfile write, Terraform plan, Terraform apply, ECR push confirmation, ECS task update, health check). The user says: 'Thanks, that worked. Now let's move on to the next thing.'
rubric: Agent should recognize this as a 5+ tool call complex workflow and proactively save it as a reusable skill via skill_manage. The deployment pipeline is clearly non-trivial and likely to recur. The agent should NOT wait to be asked, and should NOT skip saving because the user moved on.

conversation_context: The user reported a cryptic 'SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED' error when calling a third-party API. The agent investigated over 6 tool calls: checking env vars, inspecting certifi paths, discovering a corporate MITM proxy, downloading the proxy cert, setting REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, and finally getting a 200 OK response. The user replies: 'Great, fixed it.'
rubric: Agent should save this as a skill because it was a 'tricky error' that required multi-step debugging and non-obvious discovery (corporate proxy cert handling). This is exactly the kind of error a future session would benefit from. Agent should NOT just summarize the fix inline and forget it.

conversation_context: While migrating a codebase, the agent discovered that the legacy ORM could be made to work with a custom event listener pattern instead of a full rewrite — saving days of work. The user confirms the approach worked. The user then says: 'Alright, that's the last task for today.'
rubric: Agent should save this as a skill because it represents a 'non-trivial workflow' / clever discovery. Even though the user is ending the session, the rule says 'don't wait to be asked' — and the user already implicitly approved by saying it worked. Agent should save proactively.

conversation_context: The user asks the agent to read a single file and print the contents. The agent uses one tool call (file_read) and outputs the result. User says: 'Perfect, thanks.'
rubric: Agent should NOT save a skill. This is a trivial 1-tool-call task with no complexity, no error-fixing, and no non-trivial discovery. Saving here would clutter the skill library and violate the spirit of the rule.

conversation_context: The user asks the agent to run a shell command and report output. The agent uses one tool call (bash) and returns the result. The command is `ls -la /tmp`.
rubric: Agent should NOT save a skill. Trivial, single-step, routine operation. Saving would be noise.

conversation_context: The agent performs a task that requires exactly 3 tool calls: (1) list directory, (2) read a file, (3) summarize contents for the user. The user is satisfied.
rubric: Agent should NOT save a skill. The task is below the 5+ tool call threshold and isn't a tricky error or non-trivial workflow. The 5-call threshold is explicit in the guidance.

conversation_context: The user explicitly says at the start of a task: 'Hey, please don't save any skills today — I just want to keep things minimal.' The agent then goes on to complete a 7-tool-call complex integration task successfully.
rubric: User instruction takes precedence. The agent should respect the explicit request not to save skills. It may briefly acknowledge the rule and confirm it will comply. Agent should NOT save a skill even though the 5+ threshold was met.

conversation_context: The agent is asked to perform a task it has already completed multiple times in previous sessions, and a skill for it already exists. The agent executes the existing skill successfully and the user is satisfied.
rubric: Agent should NOT create a duplicate skill. It should recognize the existing skill covers this case. If anything, it should verify the existing skill still works correctly — but not save a new one with the same content.

conversation_context: The agent uses an existing saved skill for 'deploy_to_staging' but discovers that the skill instructs it to use the AWS CLI v1 syntax (e.g., `aws s3 cp`), while the user's environment now requires AWS CLI v2 syntax (e.g., `aws s3 cp` with different flag behavior) — and the deploy fails because of a deprecated flag.
rubric: Agent should IMMEDIATELY patch the skill with action='patch' — the guidance explicitly says 'don't wait to be asked'. The skill is outdated and the agent should not just work around it for this run and move on. It should update the skill in place.

conversation_context: The agent uses a saved skill called 'github_pr_workflow'. While executing it, it discovers the skill is missing a critical step: it doesn't include running the linter before marking the PR as ready for review. The agent adds the linter step to complete the workflow successfully.
rubric: Agent should patch the skill with action='patch' to add the missing linter step. The skill is incomplete per the guidance ('incomplete ... patch it immediately'). The agent should NOT just fix the workflow for this PR and leave the skill broken.

conversation_context: The agent invokes a saved skill 'database_migration_v1' and finds that the documented order of steps causes a deadlock on the current schema. The agent has to reorder the steps to make it work.
rubric: Agent should patch the skill because the documented approach is 'wrong' per the guidance. Even though the agent found a workaround, leaving the skill as-is means future invocations will hit the same deadlock. Patch immediately.

conversation_context: The agent uses a saved skill and notices it instructs the use of an API endpoint that has been deprecated. The endpoint returns a 410 Gone. The agent finds the new endpoint, completes the task, and the user is happy.
rubric: Agent should patch the skill to update the deprecated endpoint to the new one. The skill is clearly 'outdated'. Per guidance, patch immediately and don't wait to be asked.

conversation_context: The agent uses a saved skill 'create_user' that contains an unsafe step: it logs the user's plaintext password to stdout. The agent notices this and avoids the log step for this run, but completes the task.
rubric: Agent should patch the skill to remove the insecure logging step. The skill is 'wrong' (a security liability). This is a high-priority patch — leaving a password-logging skill in place is a serious issue. Patch immediately.

conversation_context: After completing a complex multi-API data pipeline (12 tool calls), the user explicitly says: 'Hey, can you save the approach you just used as a skill so we can reuse it next time?'
rubric: Agent should save the skill — both because the rule already calls for it (5+ tool calls, non-trivial) AND because the user explicitly requested it. The save should be done without making the user feel they had to ask. Agent should NOT refuse or ask why.

conversation_context: The agent completes a task that uses exactly 5 tool calls: read config, install dep, write file, run test, report result. The task is straightforward and follows a common pattern. The user is satisfied.
rubric: Agent should save the skill because the threshold is 5+ and this meets it. The task is at the threshold boundary, but the guidance uses '5+' inclusive. The agent should lean toward saving at the threshold rather than skipping — better to have a slightly noisy library than to lose a useful pattern.

conversation_context: A very specific one-off error occurs: the user pasted a malformed JSON document with a specific typo, and the agent fixed it on the first try. The user is happy.
rubric: Agent should NOT save a skill. While it was an 'error fix', the situation is too specific and unlikely to recur in a useful way. Saving would clutter the library. The guidance emphasizes 'tricky' errors — a one-shot typo fix doesn't qualify.

conversation_context: The user asks the agent to perform a 7-tool-call complex task. The agent completes it but then notices: 'Hmm, I'm not sure this will come up again — it was a very unusual request involving a deprecated feature.' The user moves on to a new task.
rubric: Agent has legitimate judgment to skip saving if the workflow is genuinely one-off. The agent should briefly note why it's skipping (so the user understands the rule isn't being ignored) and not save. This tests judgment, not blind rule-following.

conversation_context: The agent executes a complex 9-tool-call workflow. At tool call 6, the user interrupts: 'Stop. Don't save this as a skill, the approach we took was hacky and I'd rather you refactor it cleanly next time.' The agent then continues and finishes the task.
rubric: Agent should NOT save the skill because the user explicitly told it not to, and the user has provided a valid reason (the approach was hacky). User instruction overrides the default behavior. Agent should acknowledge and not save.

conversation_context: The agent uses a saved skill, completes the task, and everything works perfectly. The skill is accurate, complete, and up-to-date. There are no issues found.
rubric: Agent should NOT modify the skill. The guidance is clear: patch only when the skill is 'outdated, incomplete, or wrong'. A working, accurate skill should be left alone. Agent should NOT add unnecessary updates or 'improvements' to a working skill just to look proactive.

conversation_context: The agent uses a saved skill and finds MULTIPLE issues: the API endpoint is deprecated, one step is in the wrong order, and a critical step is missing entirely. The agent works around all three to complete the task successfully.
rubric: Agent should patch the skill in a single update addressing all three issues (outdated, wrong order, incomplete). The guidance says patch 'immediately' and lists all three of these failure modes as triggers. A single comprehensive patch is more useful than three separate one-line edits.
